summary: Poland announced temporary flight restrictions along its borders with Belarus and Ukraine. Ukraine reported a localized ceasefire near the Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant to repair a power line, while Russian forces claimed the ability to launch up to 100 ballistic missiles monthly. Multiple drone and Shahed attacks continued across several fronts, causing civilian casualties and infrastructure damage.
key_points: Poland will restrict low‑ and medium‑altitude flights along its borders with Belarus and Ukraine from 10 June to 10 September for national security reasons (Source: @UkraineNow/81670).
A sixth localized ceasefire began near the Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant to allow repairs of the Dneprovskaya power line, which has been offline for over two months (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30699).

Ukrainian intelligence reports Russia can launch up to 100 ballistic missiles per month, maintaining a steady production rate of 55‑60 units and planning delivery of up to 60 Kinzhal hypersonic missiles this year (Source: @UkraineNow/81671).
Ukrainian drone units seized fire control of the Donetsk Airport area, destroying Russian launch installations, vehicles and crew on the runway (Source: @UkraineNow/81679).
Russian attacks on Zaporizhzhia injured ten people, damaging a residential block and a multi‑storey building (Source: @UkraineNow/81681).
An explosion at a postal sorting facility in Kyiv’s Obolonsky district killed one person and injured two others (Source: @UkraineNow/81687).
Ukrainian radar reported 13 Shahed loitering munitions over the Chornomorsk‑Limanok area, indicating intensified southern‑front aerial threats (Source: @mon1tor_ua/67491).

sections.executive_summary: Poland’s new flight restrictions affect low‑altitude operations near its eastern borders. Ukraine has entered a localized ceasefire near Zaporizhzhia NPP to repair critical power infrastructure. Russian missile production remains high, with up to 100 ballistic missiles possible each month.

points: Ukrainian forces took fire control of the Donetsk Airport area, using attack drones to destroy Russian launchers, vehicles and crew directly on the runway, marking a first‑of‑its‑kind operation (Source: @UkraineNow/81679).

A localized ceasefire near the Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant enabled repair crews to work on the Dneprovskaya power line, which had been offline for more than two months (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30699).
Ukrainian air‑defence units reported intercepting multiple Shahed loitering munitions over the Chornomorsk‑Limanok sector, reflecting heightened southern‑front pressure (Source: @mon1tor_ua/67491).

Ukrainian radar and ground observers logged several UAV groups from the north of Sumy heading toward Konotop, and from the south of Kharkiv toward Barvinkove, indicating coordinated drone activity across multiple axes (Source: @eRadarrua/95492; @kpszsu/64075).
Ukrainian authorities confirmed an explosion at a Kyiv postal sorting centre in the Obolonsky district, resulting in one fatality and two injuries (Source: @UkraineNow/81687).

Russian military intelligence (GUR) assessed that Russia can launch up to 100 ballistic missiles per month, with a steady monthly production of 55‑60 missiles and plans to deliver up to 60 Kinzhal hypersonic missiles this year (Source: @UkraineNow/81671).
Russian forces conducted a drone strike on Zaporizhzhia, damaging a residential area and causing ten civilian injuries (Source: @UkraineNow/81681).
Russian tactical aviation launched guided aerial bombs over northern Kharkiv region, indicating continued use of conventional air‑to‑ground munitions (Source: @kpszsu/64103).
Reports indicated a reactive UAV approaching Zaporizhzhia from the south, suggesting ongoing Russian UAV deployment in the southern theater (Source: @kpszsu/64094).

Russian authorities noted the delivery of 14 children from a camp in Tver region to an infectious disease ward, reflecting domestic security measures unrelated to the front (Source: @tass_agency/379259).
Ukrainian observers recorded a group of five UAVs originating from the sea heading north‑west, likely targeting eastern coastal installations (Source: @eRadarrua/95504).
Shahed attacks were reported near the Donetsk airport area, with Ukrainian forces claiming they prevented the airport’s use as a launch base (Source: @UkraineNow/81679).
Russian tactical bombers released guided bombs over northern Kharkiv, supporting ground operations in the eastern sector (Source: @kpszsu/64103).
Ukrainian radar logged a cluster of UAVs from the north of Sumy moving toward Konotop, indicating pressure on the northeastern axis (Source: @eRadarrua/95492).
A reactive UAV was sighted heading toward Zaporizhzhia from the south, suggesting cross‑front coordination (Source: @kpszsu/64094).
Ukrainian sources reported UAV activity near Chernihiv, specifically near Nizhyn and Korop, and in Kharkiv near Bohodukhiv (Source: @eRadarrua/95489).
A group of UAVs from the north of Sumy was observed heading to Konotop, highlighting northern pressure (Source: @eRadarrua/95492).
Controlled UAVs were noted over Chernihiv region near Slavutych, indicating continued aerial surveillance (Source: @eRadarrua/95477).
Ukrainian radar detected guided aerial bomb releases over northern Kharkiv, reflecting Russian air‑strike activity in the north (Source: @kpszsu/64103).
Shahed clusters were reported moving from the Poltava‑Chernihiv border toward the Kyiv region, increasing threat to the capital’s northern approaches (Source: @mon1tor_ua/67485).
Russian Shahed attacks struck Brovary in Kyiv region, killing one civilian and injuring four, targeting a food‑industry facility (Source: @UkraineNow/81692).
Ukrainian radar logged 13 Shahed loitering munitions over the Chornomorsk‑Limanok area, indicating intensified southern‑front aerial threats (Source: @mon1tor_ua/67491).
A reactive UAV was observed heading toward Zaporizhzhia from the south, suggesting ongoing Russian UAV operations in the southern theater (Source: @kpszsu/64094).
Ukrainian forces reported a group of UAVs from the south of Kharkiv moving toward Barvinkove and Lozova, showing southern pressure on the east (Source: @kpszsu/64075).
Four Shahed drones were reported over the Dnipro river area, adding to the aerial threat in central‑southern Ukraine (Source: @mon1tor_ua/67488).
Ukrainian drone units seized fire control of Donetsk Airport, destroying Russian launchers and aircraft on the runway (Source: @UkraineNow/81679).
Russian tactical aviation deployed guided aerial bombs over northern Kharkiv, indicating continued air‑to‑ground operations (Source: @kpszsu/64103).

Multiple Shahed loitering munitions were detected over Kyiv’s outskirts, Brovary, and the Chornomorsk‑Limanok area, reflecting persistent low‑altitude threats (Source: @UkraineNow/81692; @mon1tor_ua/67491).
Ukrainian radar reported UAV groups from the sea heading north‑west, suggesting possible maritime‑to‑land strike vectors (Source: @eRadarrua/95504).
Russian missile production capacity remains high, with up to 100 ballistic missiles possible per month and ongoing Kinzhal hypersonic missile deliveries (Source: @UkraineNow/81671).

key_developments: Poland will restrict low‑ and medium‑altitude flights along its borders with Belarus and Ukraine from 10 June to 10 September for national security reasons (Source: @UkraineNow/81670).
A sixth localized ceasefire began near the Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant to allow repairs of the Dneprovskaya power line, which has been offline for over two months (Source: @ukr_leaks_eng/30699).
Ukrainian forces seized fire control of the Donetsk Airport area, using attack drones to destroy Russian launch installations, vehicles and crew on the runway (Source: @UkraineNow/81679).

Russian intelligence (GUR) estimates Russia can launch up to 100 ballistic missiles per month, maintaining a production rate of 55‑60 missiles and planning delivery of up to 60 Kinzhal hypersonic missiles this year (Source: @UkraineNow/81671).
Russian drone attacks on Zaporizhzhia injured ten civilians and damaged residential structures (Source: @UkraineNow/81681).
An explosion at a Kyiv postal sorting centre in the Obolonsky district killed one person and injured two others (Source: @UkraineNow/81687).
Ukrainian radar reported 13 Shahed loitering munitions over the Chornomorsk‑Limanok sector, indicating heightened southern‑front aerial threats (Source: @mon1tor_ua/67491).
sections.threat_assessment: Russian missile production and deployment capacity remains robust, with a reported ability to launch up to 100 ballistic missiles monthly and ongoing Kinzhal deliveries, posing a sustained strategic threat. UAV and Shahed activity is widespread across all fronts, targeting both civilian infrastructure and military assets, complicating Ukrainian air‑defence efforts. Ukrainian counter‑drone operations, exemplified by the seizure of Donetsk Airport, demonstrate growing proficiency but face an increasingly dense and diversified aerial threat environment.
sections.regional_response: Poland's temporary flight restrictions reflect heightened security concerns along its eastern borders, potentially limiting NATO reconnaissance and support flights. Ukraine continues to rely on localized ceasefires to protect critical energy infrastructure, while seeking to neutralize drone threats through integrated air‑defence and offensive UAV operations. International partners, including the United States and NATO, are monitoring Russian missile escalations, influencing diplomatic and aid decisions.
sections.intelligence_notes: Open‑source monitoring indicates a surge in low‑altitude UAV activity from maritime, northern, and southern vectors, suggesting coordinated Russian efforts to exploit gaps in Ukrainian air‑defence coverage. The concentration of Shahed attacks in the southern and central regions aligns with reported Russian attempts to disrupt logistics and civilian morale. Continued observation of missile production figures and launch patterns is essential to anticipate potential large‑scale strikes.
